attachment.name: Attachment Styles
attachment.subtitle: How you manage closeness and distance in relationships
attachment.description: Based on the ECR-R model (Experiences in Close Relationships). Measures two dimensions: Anxiety (fear of abandonment) and Avoidance (discomfort with intimacy).

questions.text: I feel uncomfortable when someone gets emotionally close to me.
I prefer not to show my partner how I really feel.
When my partner wants a lot of intimacy, I feel tense.
I find it difficult to rely emotionally on my partner.
I prefer not to depend on the people I love.
I'm not comfortable sharing my deepest thoughts with others.
I'm afraid of becoming too attached to my partner.
I find it hard to let partners get emotionally close to me.
I avoid opening up even when I genuinely care about the person.
I feel uncomfortable when I'm asked for direct emotional support.
I'm afraid of being abandoned by my partner.
I need frequent confirmation that my partner loves me.
When my partner is unavailable, I feel anxious.
I often worry that my partner is interested in someone else.
I fear my partner doesn't care about me as much as I care about them.
I worry about not being interesting enough for my partner.
When I don't hear from my partner for a few hours, I feel uneasy.
The idea that my partner might leave me deeply distresses me.
I get nervous if my partner doesn't respond to messages quickly.
I often need to feel that my partner is satisfied with our relationship.

loveLanguages.name: Love Languages
loveLanguages.subtitle: How you prefer to give and receive affection
loveLanguages.description: Based on Gary Chapman's model. Identifies your primary love language among the 5 love languages.

questions.text: I feel loved when my partner explicitly tells me how much they care about me.
Sincere compliments recharge me emotionally more than any gesture.
A verbal insult hurts me more than an unkind action.
I feel loved when my partner dedicates time just to me, without phone or distractions.
The most important thing for me is that my partner is present, not what we do.
I feel neglected when my partner seems elsewhere even if physically here.
When my partner brings me even a small unexpected gift, it makes me feel special.
For me, gifts aren't objects — they're visible symbols of my partner's love.
I hold on to objects or symbols that my partner has given me.
I feel loved when my partner helps me with something practical without being asked.
A partner who does things for me speaks louder than a thousand words.
I feel unloved when my partner promises to do something and doesn't follow through.
A hug, held hand, or caress calms me and makes me feel connected.
Physical contact (even non-sexual) is essential for my well-being in a relationship.
Lack of physical touch in a relationship makes me feel distant and alone.

bigFive.name: OCEAN Profile
bigFive.subtitle: Your 5 dominant personality traits
bigFive.description: Short version of the Big Five model. Measures the 5 core traits: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism.

questions.text: I'm curious and love exploring new and complex ideas.
I deeply appreciate art, music, or literature.
I often have original thoughts and a vivid imagination.
I'm open to viewpoints very different from my own.
I'm organized and methodical in how I approach things.
I always follow through on what I start, even when it gets hard.
I meet deadlines and keep commitments rigorously.
I'm detail-oriented and it bothers me to make mistakes.
In groups, I like being the center of attention.
I feel energized after socializing for a long time.
I'm naturally outgoing and talk easily with strangers.
I prefer being with others rather than alone.
I'm genuinely interested in the well-being of others.
In a conflict, I always seek a fair compromise.
I find it easy to forgive people who have hurt me.
It comes naturally to me to be considerate of others' needs.
I worry often, even about things others find trivial.
I get easily upset by stress or difficulties.
I tend to feel anxious or emotionally tense.
I sometimes feel overwhelmed by emotions.

communication.name: Communication Style
communication.subtitle: Rapport Talk vs Report Talk
communication.description: Based on Deborah Tannen's research. Identifies whether you tend to communicate to build connection (Rapport) or to exchange information and solve problems (Report).

questions.text: When a friend tells me about a problem, I share a similar experience to make them feel less alone.
In a conversation, I care more about understanding how the other feels than finding a solution.
When discussing something, the listening process feels as important as the outcome.
I often share personal things about myself to create intimacy and connection.
In discussions, I seek consensus and harmony more than winning the point.
When someone describes a problem, my instinct is to suggest practical solutions.
In conversations, I prefer to get straight to the point without detours.
I find it frustrating to talk about a problem for a long time without reaching a concrete conclusion.
In discussions, content and facts seem more important to me than emotional tone.
When someone talks to me, I focus on what they should do, not on how they feel.

conflictStyle.name: Conflict Style
conflictStyle.subtitle: How you behave when conflict arises
conflictStyle.description: Based on the Thomas-Kilmann model. Identifies your dominant style among 5 conflict approaches: Competing, Collaborating, Compromising, Avoiding, Accommodating.

questions.text: During a conflict, I keep defending my position until the other person acknowledges I'm right.
In a heated discussion, I don't back down until I get what I want.
I think it's important not to lose ground in arguments with my partner.
I defend my point of view firmly even under strong pressure.
I prefer to impose my solution rather than accept one that doesn't fully convince me.
In a conflict, I don't stop until we find a solution that truly satisfies both of us.
I invest time and energy in understanding the other's needs to find a creative agreement.
Facing a conflict together feels like an opportunity to understand each other better.
I actively explore the other's reasons before defending my own.
In a discussion, I seek a win-win solution even if it takes more time.
In a conflict, I look for a middle ground even if it's not ideal.
I'm willing to give something up if the other does the same.
I prefer a partial agreement to a prolonged conflict.
In a discussion, I seek the fastest solution that satisfies both of us at least partially.
I find it acceptable that neither gets everything, as long as we reach a balance.
When conflict arises, I prefer to avoid the topic until things calm down.
If I can, I postpone difficult discussions to a better moment.
In an argument, I tend to physically withdraw or change the subject.
I prefer to endure in silence rather than face an open conflict.
When there's tension, I step back and wait for it to pass on its own.
In a conflict, I often give in to the other's requests to keep the peace.
I set aside my needs to make my partner feel good, even at my own expense.
I easily adapt to the other's position even if I don't fully agree.
For me, preserving the relationship matters more than defending my position.
I willingly give something up to avoid tension, even when I'm right.

apologyLanguages.name: Apology Languages
apologyLanguages.subtitle: How you give and receive genuine apologies
apologyLanguages.description: Based on Gary Chapman's model. Identifies what makes an apology credible and acceptable to you among 5 languages: Regret, Responsibility, Restitution, Repentance, Forgiveness.

questions.text: When someone apologizes, the most important thing is that they genuinely show they feel my pain.
An 'I'm truly sorry, I hurt you' means more to me than anything else.
I don't feel forgiven unless I see the other person is genuinely emotionally sorry.
When I apologize, explicitly acknowledging my mistake is the most important part.
I can't accept an apology if the other person doesn't openly recognize what they did wrong.
'I was wrong, it's my fault' is the foundation of any apology I can accept.
Apologies are only credible to me when accompanied by a concrete act of repair.
I expect whoever wronged me to do something concrete to make it right, not just words.
'What can I do to make it up to you?' is the part of an apology that matters most to me.
To accept an apology, I need to believe the other will actually do something differently going forward.
'I won't do it again' is the part of an apology that carries the most weight for me.
It's hard for me to truly forgive unless I see real behavioral change.
Having someone explicitly ask 'do you forgive me?' is important for me to close the conflict.
Forgiveness must be explicitly requested — I don't give it automatically.
A clear 'please forgive me' helps me let go of resentment and move forward.

careStyle.name: Care Style
careStyle.subtitle: How you support the people you love when they struggle
careStyle.description: Identifies your natural approach to caring for others. Four main styles: Emotional (empathy and listening), Practical (concrete solutions), Presence (being there), Autonomy (space and trust).

questions.text: When someone I love is struggling, my first instinct is to understand how they feel.
I prefer to listen and empathize rather than propose solutions when someone is suffering.
I focus first on validating the other's emotions, before moving to facts or solutions.
I only truly feel helpful when the other person feels emotionally understood.
When someone has a problem, my natural impulse is to find a concrete solution.
Helping means doing something useful — solving, organizing, fixing.
I feel most useful when I can do something practical for someone going through a hard time.
I prefer to act rather than talk when someone needs support.
Being close to someone — even in silence — is my primary form of support.
When a friend is struggling, the most important thing for me is just to be there, even without saying anything.
In times of crisis, I worry about never leaving my loved ones alone.
My constant physical or emotional presence is worth more than any words or actions.
I respect others' need to face problems in their own way, without interference.
The best support I can give is often to step back and not intrude.
I firmly believe in giving people space to find their own answers.
Intervening too much when someone has a problem can be counterproductive — I prefer to wait.

coreValues.name: Core Values
coreValues.subtitle: What truly drives your life choices
coreValues.description: Adapted from Schwartz's model. Identifies your dominant values among 5 orientations: Security, Freedom, Achievement, Connection, Growth.

questions.text: Stability and predictability in my life are fundamental to my well-being.
I would prefer a safe and orderly life over one full of surprises.
Tradition and family rituals have deep value for me.
I need to know what to expect — uncertainty weighs on me significantly.
Autonomy and independence are non-negotiable values in my life.
I feel a strong pull toward new experiences, travel, and adventure.
I must be able to make free choices without feeling constrained by others' expectations.
Living by my own rules — not society's — is essential to me.
Professional success is one of the most important priorities in my life.
I'm motivated by ambitious goals and the challenge of achieving them.
Recognition for what I accomplish carries significant weight for me.
Investing in my professional development is something I would never put on the back burner.
Relationships with people I care about are at the heart of what makes me happy.
Taking care of others and contributing to their lives has deep meaning for me.
Family — biological or chosen — is my primary anchor.
Being part of a community that shares my values is important to me.
Growing as a person — learning, evolving, improving — is one of my core drives.
I constantly seek new perspectives that challenge my existing beliefs.
I feel fulfilled when I sense I'm becoming a better version of myself.
The sense that my life has meaning and direction is essential to my well-being.
